data.title: Craft Sportswear Signs Partnership Agreement with IFA New England
data.description:
Intercontinental Football Academy (IFA) New England, Challenger Teamwear, and Craft Sportswear North America have signed a multi-year partnership agreement to provide IFA New England's players and coaches with match kits and training apparel. This collaboration unites three organizations with a shared commitment and dedication to delivering top-quality products, excellent service, and a first-class experience for athletes.

Intercontinental Football Academy (IFA) New England is one of the largest soccer clubs in the USA, with approximately 4,000 active youth players. The agreement spans five years.

The agreement marks the first step in New Wave Group's teamwear initiative in North America.

- In recent years, we have developed a teamwear offering for various sports in the European market. It is exciting to see that our strategy also fits the American market, and after just three months on the continent, one of the largest soccer clubs in the USA has chosen Craft as a partner for the next five years, says Stefan Persson, CEO of Craft of Scandinavia AB.

About Craft Sportswear

Craft Sportswear operates in 70 markets worldwide. In soccer and team sports, the brand has agreements with thousands of clubs and collaborates with clubs such as IFK Göteborg, Hammarby IF, Trelleborgs FF, Varbergs Bois, Växjö DFF, KAA Gent (Belgium), Fredrikstad (Norway), AGF Århus (Denmark), Almere City (Netherlands), SV Darmstadt 98 (Germany), AC Oulo, and Ekenäs IF (Finland). Additionally, Craft collaborates with the Swedish national handball team and 15 clubs in the top tier handball leagues, more than ten elite basketball teams, the Swedish and Danish national volleyball teams, among others. Craft also partners with the Swedish, Norwegian, and French national cross-country skiing teams, the Swedish national table tennis team, the Danish and Finnish national athletics teams, and the Norwegian and French national biathlon teams. Craft Sportswear is part of the New Wave Group.

About Challenger Teamwear

Challenger Teamwear, headquartered in Lenexa, Kansas, offers a comprehensive range of sportswear and equipment, providing personalized and efficient solutions for sports organizations across the USA. With a commitment to supporting their partners and thinking outside the box, Challenger Teamwear constantly finds new ways to help teams and clubs succeed both on and off the pitch.

About Intercontinental Football Academy New England

Intercontinental Football Academy (IFA) New England is based in Massachusetts, USA. With a large, dedicated player base across the region, IFA New England offers top-class soccer training for both boys and girls, combining a player-first approach with a commitment to development. With a strong European influence and a diverse player base, IFA promotes a soccer culture rooted in quality, ambition, and innovation. Their teams compete at the highest levels and work to develop elite players while strengthening the broader soccer community.

data.title: Market Chatter: United States Steel, Nippon Steel Offer Increased Investment to White House to Close Proposed Merger
data.description:
United States Steel and Nippon Steel are discussing with White House officials ways to preserve their proposed $14 billion merger and secure billions more in investments from the Japanese steelmaker into Rust Belt sites, Semafor reported Friday, citing unnamed sources familiar with the talks.

Nippon Steel has reportedly offered to increase the $2.7 billion it previously proposed to upgrade US Steel facilities, with some saying it could be upped to as much as $7 billion.

Under the new terms, Nippon would retain its offer to acquire the US steel manufacturer for $55 per share, the people told Semafor.

US Steel, Nippon, and the White House did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' requests for comment.

US Steel's stock rose 6.3% in recent Friday premarket activity.

data.title: Nvidia-backed CoreWeave poised to debut after IPO slashed
data.description:
** Shares of CoreWeave (CRWV.O), artificial intelligence startup backed by Nvidia , set to debut on the Nasdaq with ticker "CRWV" on Fri after hotly-anticipated IPO prices well below expectations

** Livingston, New Jersey-based co late Thurs announced 37.5 mln shares at $40 for total raise of $1.5 bln, and valuing co at roughly $23 bln

** Co and some of its existing shareholders had planned to sell 49 mln shares at $47-$55, or up to $2.7 bln

** Nvidia, which has ~6% stake, reportedly buying $250 mln worth of IPO shares

** Founded in 2017 as a crypto mining firm, CoreWeave provides access to data centers and high-powered chips for AI workloads, mainly supplied by Nvidia, and competes against cloud providers like Microsoft's Azure and Amazon's AWS

** In 2024, CoreWeave's rev surged more than eightfold to $1.9 bln, though has been unprofitable. Microsoft , which is also a customer, accounted for nearly two-thirds of CoreWeave's rev last year

** Earlier this week, D.A. Davidson initiated coverage of CoreWeave at 'neutral', saying co is "the largest in the new neocloud category, but we see it mostly as a highly levered way for Microsoft to offload less desirable workloads and Nvidia to leverage a small investment into a very large customer”

** “This structure may continue to work as long as demand for AI continues to grow exponentially,” D.A. Davidson analyst Gil Luria wrote in note

** CoreWeave's IPO is seen as a critical barometer of investor appetite for new listings, and whether enthusiasm for AI-related names remains buoyant or has waned, especially since the launch of China's DeepSeek

** Morgan Stanley, JP Morgan and Goldman Sachs leading an 18-firm underwriting group for the IPO

** The offering comes as markets have been roiled by President Donald Trump's tariff salvos and concerns of a slowing U.S. economy

** Through Thurs, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq have lost 3% and ~8%, respectively, YTD. And chipmakers have been thumped, evidenced by ~11% drop in the Philadelphia semiconductor index this year, while Nvidia's stock has shed 17%

data.title: lululemon Is Maintained at Buy by Needham
WH Smith Brand Strength Was Major Asset for Any High Street Store OwnerMarket Talk
data.description: WH Smith's lower-than-expected price for its high street business reflects the fact that the brand wasn't included in the sale, AJ Bell investment director Russ Mould writes. WH Smith sold the business for 52 million pounds--implying an enterprise value of 76 million pounds--compared with speculation the company would get 100 million pounds. The brand strength was a major asset to anyone looking to keep the stores running as per normal, Mould says. He adds that the new owner will have to do something remarkable to bring in shoppers under the unfamiliar TGJones name. Shares are down 3.4% at 10.53 pounds. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

data.title: Djibouti's sovereign wealth fund aims to double assets in a decade, CEO says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Says to double assets to more than $2 billion in 10 years
  • FSD wholly owns state telecoms monopoly, 40% stake in GHIH
  • It also gets 20% cut in annual revenue from military bases

Djibouti's sovereign wealth fund plans to double its assets under management of more than $1 billion in the next decade, its chief executive said, investing in high-return projects to support the government's economic development plans.

The Horn of Africa country established the Fonds Souverain de Djibouti (FSD) in 2020, looking to tap a model that has successfully been deployed by Gulf and Asian economies like Qatar and Singapore, to bankroll economic diversification and development, while offering a cushion during crises.

"All else being the same, the base of dividends etcetera, I think we can double the size of the assets under management within the next decade certainly," Slim Feriani, the chief executive officer of FSD, told Reuters in an interview.

The wealth fund fully owns the state telecommunications monopoly Djibouti Telecom, and a 40% stake in GHIH, which is the holding company for many of Djibouti's maritime and logistical assets including its port, Feriani said.

It also gets about $25 million in cash every year, representing a 20% share from the annual revenue from leases of bases by the government to foreign militaries.

Djibouti's location makes it a strategically important country, hosting military bases for the United States, China, Japan and European nations like Italy and France, Feriani said.

Djibouti, at the southern entrance to the Red Sea, is one of the smallest countries in Africa with a population of just over 1 million people.

Its economy is heavily dependent on global maritime transport and its state-of-the-art key deep water port, which also serves the neighbouring, landlocked Ethiopia, a fast-growing market of more than 100 million people.

The World Bank forecasts economic growth to average 5.4% from 2024-2026, supported by export earnings from logistics and re-exports to Ethiopia.

"We are a hub for ports and logistics, but we should be a hub for manufacturing industry for the rest of the region," said Feriani.

FSD has an investment pipeline worth more than $50 million, encompassing data centres, renewable energy and logistics in the country, as well as a stake in a solar energy project in the Grand Bara area, said Feriani who previously held positions at investment bank Nomura in London and as a minister in Tunisia.

However, the fund would eventually also invest in assets outside the country, said Feriani, without giving a time frame.

"Our inspiration is Temasek," he said, referring to Singapore's sovereign wealth fund.

data.title: US Stocks Brief: S&P Futures Flat To Slightly Lower; Nasdaq 100 Futures Down Near 0.1% and Down Near 11% From Highs of the Year, Bloomberg TV Says
Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) export prices edge lower in the United States
data.description: This week, PVC export prices dropped marginally in the US.An industry source in the U.S. informed a Polymerupdate team member, "The US polyvinyl chloride (PVC) market experienced a price decline over the past week, primarily due to ongoing weak demand, especially from Latin America. Market participants indicate that purchasing interest is low, with no immediate signs of recovery. Consequently, industry experts largely anticipate that US PVC export prices will persist in their downward trajectory in the upcoming weeks. Traders attribute this pessimistic outlook to several factors, including sluggish global demand and strategic pricing manoeuvres by US manufacturers. The pricing strategy implemented by one US producer in April has set a precedent, leading to expectations that further price reductions may be necessary in May, as there are few viable options to boost demand. Compounding the uncertainty, discussions regarding potential tariff changes have surfaced as a significant factor affecting market sentiment. Some analysts believe that alterations in trade policies could influence export competitiveness, presenting additional hurdles for US suppliers.Moreover, competition from PVC sourced from Asia remains a significant concern, particularly along the West Coast of South America. Buyers in this region are actively seeking more affordable alternatives, with Asian suppliers providing competitively priced shipments. This intensifying competitive pressure is further limiting US export prospects, resulting in an increasingly uncertain market outlook.FAS Houston PVC export prices were assessed at USD 670-680/mt, inched lower by USD (-5/mt) from the previous week.Spot feedstock VCM prices on Friday were assessed at the USD 510-520/mt FOB US Gulf levels while FOB US Gulf export EDC prices were assessed at the USD 155-165/mt levels, both steady from the previous week.Spot upstream ethylene prices on Thursday were assessed at 23.50-24.00 cents/lb FOB U.S. Gulf, a week on week decrease of USD (-1.50 cents/lb).In Asia, PVC prices were assessed at USD 660-700/mt CFR China, constant from the previous week.

data.title: Stocks Had a Bumpy Quarter. Why Trump's Trade War Will Make Things Worse and 5 Other Things to Know Today.
data.description:
This quarter has been turbulent but markets should brace for even worse as a global trade war kicks off.

The S&P 500 has fallen 3.2% so far this year, on track to snap a five-quarter winning streak. It would be the index's worst start to a year since 2022, when it fell 5% in the first quarter and ended the year 19% lower.

That's a gloomy historical comparison but those hoping for a more serene start to the next quarter should look away now — it's going to start with a bang.

President Donald Trump has named April 2 Liberation Day, the date he will unveil reciprocal tariffs on countries around the world. But it's not just U.S. trade policy that should concern markets, it's the global retaliation too. Trump's auto tariffs sparked a stern response from world leaders, ramping up the risk of an escalating global trade war.

Canada's Prime Minister Mark Carney said "nothing is off the table," while Mexico's Claudia Sheinbaum promised an "integral response" on April 3. Japan and the European Union are also considering their responses. To make matters worse, Trump threatened the EU and Canada with larger tariffs if they work together against the U.S.

It's not hard to see how global tariffs could snowball. Inflation expectations are rising and Friday's core PCE index — the Federal Reserve's preferred metric — is a good indication of what's happening to prices.

There are reasons to be hopeful, though. The April 2 levies could end up being less severe than expected and there is scope for more positive tariff surprises on the horizon, if previously threatened taxes on semiconductors and pharmaceuticals are watered down.

And for all the damaging sentiment surveys, the hard economic data are pointing toward a resilient U.S. economy — at least for now. Jobless claims fell last week, Thursday numbers showed, the opposite of what would be expected ahead of a recession.

It's been a bad quarter and the risks are mounting but don't write off the year just yet.

  • Callum Keown

***

CoreWeave IPO Tests Appetite for New Deals

CoreWeave, a pure play AI cloud company that generates all of its revenue from cloud rentals of AI servers that use Nvidia chips, raised $1.5 billion in its initial public offering late Thursday, significantly less than expected at $40 a share. The deal was seen as testing investor appetite for new deals.

  • The stock could begin trading today at Nasdaq. The IPO, which also gauged enthusiasm for the artificial intelligence trade, had targeted a sale of 49 million shares at $47 to $55 each, raising up to $2.7 billion. Nvidia was expected to be an anchor investor in the IPO, CNBC reported.
  • CoreWeave began in 2017 as a crypto miner and shifted to supplying AI cloud services after the 2018 crypto bust. It went from $16 million in revenue in 2022, the year ChatGPT launched, to $1.9 billion in sales in 2024. Microsoft made up 62% of 2024 revenue, and Nvidia is believed to be another large customer.
  • CoreWeave also has notched a deal with AI chatbot maker OpenAI for a contract value of up to $11.6 billion, though neither would disclose the terms. Not including that deal, CoreWeave's backlog stood at $15 billion at the end of 2024, with $8 billion of that coming due in 2025 and 2026.
  • Customer concentration risk is just one challenge. CoreWeave had over $12 billion in borrowing arrangements at the end of 2024, and its $361 million in 2024 interest expenses more than offset its $324 million operating profit. Because of its capital-intense business model, the company's borrowing will likely grow from here.

What's Next: For CoreWeave, some $5.6 billion of its loans must be repaid in 2025 and 2026. It will use some of the IPO proceeds to begin that process, with $1 billion going to pay off a 12% interest loan that comes due at the end of the year.

  • Adam Levine and Janet H. Cho

***

Health Agencies Are Slashing Workforce in Broad Restructuring

President Donald Trump's Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. plans to cut another 10,000 federal workers starting today from agencies overseeing disease outbreaks, new drug approvals, and other public health services. He is also ordering a broad consolidation of agencies and regional offices.

  • On Thursday, Kennedy described the divisions under his control as a "sprawling bureaucracy" rife with waste. The latest cuts come on top of 10,000 voluntary resignations offered earlier in the Trump administration and will slash the HHS full-time workforce to 62,000.
  • Certain job functions and departments will be centralized, potentially reshaping how the agencies operate. The cuts hit the Food and Drug Administration, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, the National Institutes of Health, and the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, agencies once viewed as operating more independently.
  • The department is also combining multiple agencies into a new Administration for a Healthy America, which it said would improve the coordination of health resources for low-income households, focused on primary care, maternal and child health, and other issues.
  • Former federal officials were critical of the cuts. Zachary Dembner, a former senior communications advisor at the Administration for Strategic Preparedness and Response, which manages emergency medical stockpiles and the nation's pandemic preparedness, said folding that division into the CDC is a "step backwards" for preparedness.

What's Next: HHS said FDA staffing cuts won't affect employees reviewing new drugs or medical devices. Trump healthcare nominees still awaiting confirmation include former TV host Dr. Mehmet Oz to lead the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, and acting director Susan Monarez to lead the CDC.

  • Josh Nathan-Kazis and Janet H. Cho

***

BP CEO's Plan to Catch Exxon and Chevron

The Gulf of Mexico — or, as some oil executives now call it, the Gulf of America — is critical to BP CEO Murray Auchincloss's plans to win back investor confidence and fend off a powerful activist in Elliott Management, he told Barron's in an interview in London.

  • BP has a history in the Gulf -- it's long been a leading producer with cutting-edge wells, and it's also the site of the firm's most devastating event. The 2010 Deepwater Horizon disaster killed 11 workers and ultimately cost some $65 billion.
  • The advantage is that BP has already invested heavily there. Now it's time for the money that the company put into the ground to start paying off. Big jumps in cash flow can then be used for dividends and buybacks.
  • "We've got top in the sector -- a 20% compound annual cash flow growth rate over the next three years -- and an underpriced share price," Auchincloss said. He's confident he can extend those superior growth rates beyond 2027.
  • BP has lagged behind rivals Shell, Exxon, and Chevron for the past five years. It reset its strategy last month to try to catch up.

What's Next: The big question is whether investors, and Elliott in particular, will buy the pitch. Elliott, which declined to comment, has a record of pushing for executive changes and aggressive strategies at energy companies. It may make its demands on BP public as the Annual General Meeting approaches on April 17.

  • Brian Swint

***

Bezos, Gates Pledge Billions of Dollars for Child Nutrition

Nearly a dozen foundations and philanthropists including Amazon founder Jeff Bezos and his parents, Jackie and Miguel Bezos, the Gates Foundation, and the Rockefeller Foundation, have pledged more than $2 billion to address global child malnutrition. The pledges come as the Trump administration attempts steep cuts to international nutrition aid.

  • The financial commitments were announced in Paris at a Nutrition for Growth Summit event co-hosted by the Washington nonprofit Stronger Foundations for Nutrition, which works with philanthropies to direct funding to nutritional interventions, and the OECD Network of Foundations Working for Development.
  • The wealthy donors view their contributions as creating space for other entities to step in with funding, including governments, philanthropists, and development financial institutions. The Gates Foundation, long focused on improving nutrition for mothers and children, made an additional $750 million commitment over four years.
  • The Bezos family plans to donate up to $500 million to Unicef USA for the Child Nutrition Fund, a UNICEF-led program. Jeff Bezos and his parents each pledged $250 million. The fund matches every dollar a government spends to buy therapeutic nutrition for women and children with a $2 donation.
  • Elon Musk, the billionaire Tesla CEO who is advising President Donald Trump on cutting federal spending, was the world's wealthiest person for the fourth time in five years, with $420 billion as of mid-January, the Hurun Report said. Worldwide, the number of billionaires is up 5% to 3,442.

What's Next: The government's efforts to dismantle the U.S. Agency for International Development, which administers global food aid, was blocked by a Maryland federal judge on March 18 as "likely unconstitutional." But the government has appealed the decision and the future of food aid remains uncertain.

  • Abby Schultz

***

U.S. Tariffs Put American Farmers on the Front Line Rural America sent President Donald Trump back to the White House. Now, Trump's new trade war is putting its loyalty to the test. Their products are almost certain to be targeted by other countries retaliating against American goods. Tariffs and government downsizing are also raising costs for farmers.

  • The Agriculture Department expects U.S. farmers to export $170.5 billion for the year ending in September. Trump's trade war enters its next phase on April 2, when the White House unveils tariffs on goods from countries that have higher trade barriers than the U.S. Agricultural products are expected to be in the mix.
  • China, Canada, and Mexico are American farmers' largest foreign markets, and the three countries have implemented levies on nearly $30 billion in U.S. agricultural exports since Trump's term began, according to the American Farm Bureau Federation, or about 17% of 2024's exports.
  • Increased protectionism might help some farmers, but others probably won't be able to find enough domestic buyers to fill demand gaps. In 2019, the U.S. imported more agricultural products than it exported for the first time in nearly six decades as Americans craved more coffee, cocoa, and certain distilled spirits.
  • Some American products have almost no domestic markets. For example, China is one of the biggest importers of U.S. pork variety meats, including hearts, livers, tongues, and other organs that Americans aren't likely to embrace. In March, China put an additional 10% tariff on such imports, bringing the total to 47%.

What's Next: Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins has said the administration is considering financial support for farmers. Farm aid could mirror a $28 billion program that the Trump administration implemented during the China trade war beginning in 2018. For more read here.

  • Joe Light

***

Do you remember this week's news? Take our quiz below to test your knowledge. Tell us how you did in an email to thebarronsdaily@barrons.com.

1. A study by the Commonwealth Fund and George Washington University found that Republican-sought cuts to Medicaid and supplemental nutrition programs would result in the loss of one million jobs and cost local and state economies how much in lost tax revenue next year?

a. $4.8 billion b. $6.8 billion c. $8.8 billion d. None of the above

2. The Conference Board's consumer confidence index has dropped 20 points since the November election as consumers grow increasingly wary about the outlook. Its March reading was the lowest in how long?

a. Two years b. Four years c. Six years d. Eight years

3. President Donald Trump announced 25% tariffs on all cars imported to the U.S. plus key car parts. Which of the following auto makers is seen as coming out ahead in the trade war?

a. Tesla b. General Motors c. Ford Motor d. Stellantis

4. United Airlines has become the latest carrier to tweak its business model by adding a bunch of perks to its rewards credit cards. Which of the following is it adding?

a. Car rentals b. Rideshare credits c. Hotel awards d. All of the above

5. Walt Disney's latest live-action film wasn't a rousing success at the box office, bringing in about $43 million domestically in its opening weekend. Which of the following was that?

a. Lilo & Stitch b. Moana c. Snow White d. Tangled

Answers: 1( c); 2( b ); 3( a); 4( d ); 5( c )

data.title: Woodbois Raises Nearly GBP2.7 Million via Share Subscription
data.description:
Woodbois said Friday it raised 2.7 million pounds sterling via subscription of 5,305,000,000 new ordinary shares at 0.0005 pounds apiece.

The forestry company issued 2,652,500,000 warrants with a strike price of 0.00125 pounds and two subscription options were granted under the same terms for 1.3 billion ordinary shares and 650 million warrants.

Meanwhile, the company conditionally issued 358 million new ordinary shares to Akira GmBH and one of its brokers to settle commission fees. Akira GmBH also received 127 million warrants at a strike price of 0.00125 pounds.

data.title: Mexico's Central Bank Delivers A 50bps Cut, Leaves Doors Open to Another Same-Sized Cut in May, Notes BBVA Research
data.description:
Mexico's central bank (Banxico) lowered the policy rate by 50 bps to 9.00% on Thursday, noted BBVA Research.

The statement not only kept a strong dovish tone, but also added some dovish tweaks that signal growing confidence among Board members about the inflation outlook, said BBVA Research.

Banxico still estimates that inflation will reach 3.0% at some point in 2026, as opposed to BBVA Research's view that inflation is more likely to hover around 3.5%.

In a context of rising growth concerns and lower inflation concerns, Banxico left the door wide open to a third straight 50bps cut in May, added BBVA Research.

data.description:
G Mining Ventures Reports Fourth Quarter & Year-End 2024 Results

PR Newswire

BROSSARD, QC, March 28, 2025

(All amounts are in USD unless stated otherwise)

BROSSARD, QC, March 28, 2025 /PRNewswire/ - G Mining Ventures Corp. ("GMIN" or the "Corporation" or "we") is pleased to report its production and financial results(1) for the fourth quarter and full year ended December 31, 2024.

Fourth Quarter 2024 Highlights First Full Quarter of Commercial Production

  • Production: 40,147 ounces ("oz") of gold ("Au") in Doré
    -- Gold sold totaled 39,938 oz
  • Operating Costs: All-in sustaining costs2 ("AISC") of $862 per oz Au sold
  • Net Income: $47.6 million, or $0.21 per share -- basic
  • Adjusted Net Income2: $58.0 million or $0.26 per share -- basic
  • Adjusted EBITDA (Earnings before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation and Amortization)2: $77.9 million
  • Cash Flow from Operating Activities: $73.2 million before the net change in non-cash working capital items
  • Free Cash Flow2: $53.0 million, or $0.24 per share -- basic
  • Cash and Cash Equivalents: $141.2 million

"2024 marked a transformational year for GMIN as we executed all phases of our 'Buy. Build. Operate.' Strategy," commented Louis-Pierre Gignac, President & Chief Executive Officer. "With just four months of commercial production, TZ is already generating significant free cash flow, giving us the financial flexibility to fund our next phase of growth. The acquisition of Oko West, recognized as one of the top undeveloped gold projects globally, positions us to surpass 500,000 ounces of annual production. Combined with the high-potential Gurupi project acquired from BHP, GMIN is firmly established as a low-cost, high-growth gold producer with a clear path to long-term value creation."

Louis-Pierre Gignac continued, "With a full year of production coming out of TZ in 2025, we expect strong cash flow to support disciplined investment in Oko West and strategic exploration across our portfolio. Key milestones--Oko West's Feasibility Study, permitting, and funding--are on track to unlock our next phase of growth. GMIN remains focused on low-cost production, project execution, and advancing our multi-asset platform."

Liquidity and Capital Resources

The Corporation ended 2024 with a cash and cash equivalents balance of $141.2 million.

The $36.6 million increase quarter over quarter is attributed to the following:

  • Free Cash Flow generated in Q4 totals $53.0 million
  • Investments in Long Term Inventories total $17.0 million and $18.8 million in Non-Sustaining Capital
  • Net financing inflows total $13.3 million, driven largely by $15.5 million of warrant proceeds
  • Positive foreign exchange adjustment $6.1 million

Free Cash Flow generated by TZ will be the primary source of capital to fund future disciplined growth at GMIN's development projects and is defined by the Corporation as:

  • Cash Flow from Operating Activities; adjusted for
  • Investments in Long Term Inventories, which represents ore mined and stockpiled that will not be processed in the following 12-month period5; less

Tocantinzinho Mine Highlights After two months of successful commissioning, GMIN declared commercial production at TZ on September 1st, 2024, delivered on schedule and within budget. Q4 marked TZ's first full quarter of commercial production, contributing 40,147 ounces to an annual production total of 63,566 ounces.

Mining operations performed ahead of expectations, with a total of 14.3 Mt mined in 2024, including 6.4 Mt of ore, resulting in a low annual strip ratio of 1.23x. Plant throughput in Q4 averaged 10,523 t/d (82% of nameplate capacity) despite unplanned downtime related to SAG mill liner replacements. Recoveries remained strong at over 89.2%, in line with Feasibility Study estimates, supporting the processing of higher-grade ore averaging 1.45 g/t Au in Q4. A full metallic liner system, scheduled for installation in early Q2 2025, is expected to improve plant availability and allow the process plant to reach nameplate capacity.

In its first full quarter of commercial production in Q4, cash costs at TZ were $577/oz and AISC was $862/oz. For the full year, cash costs were $668/oz and AISC was $972/oz.

Annual owner-operated mining costs averaged $2.31/t mined ($2.85/t in Q4), while processing costs were $10.02/t milled ($10.14/t in Q4). General and administrative ("G&A") expenses averaged $7.29/t milled for the year, and lowered to $6.62/t in Q4. As the operation continues to ramp up toward nameplate capacity, unit G&A costs are expected to decline due to the fixed nature of a significant portion of these expenses.

TZ is a major employer of local workforce, with 67% of the 1,000 employees and contractors coming from local communities, and 97% Brazilians.

Oko West Project Highlights

In July 2024, GMIN completed the business combination with Reunion Gold to acquire the high-quality Oko West gold project located in Guyana. In September 2024, GMIN reported results from a positive Preliminary Economic Assessment ("PEA") on Oko West, demonstrating after-tax Net Present Value (5%) of $1.4 billion, Internal Rate of Return of 21% and a payback period of 3.8 years using a $1,950/oz base case gold price. The average annual gold production is estimated to be 353,000 ounces at an AISC of $986/oz over a 12.7-year mine life. The initial capital cost is estimated to be $936 million, with sustaining capital costs of $537 million over the life of mine (see news release dated September 9, 2024).

During 2024, a 49,000m definition drilling program was completed at Oko West in support of the upcoming Feasibility Study ("FS"). Additionally, a new regional exploration drilling program began at Oko West to investigate structures identified through geophysics and geochemical soil anomalies.

The Environmental Impact Assessment and Environmental Impact Statement submissions (jointly, "ESIA") for Oko West were made to the Guyana Environmental Protection Agency ("EPA") in November 2024.

After receiving an Interim Environmental Permit on Oko West from EPA in January 2025, GMIN announced the commencement of early works construction in March 2025. Site preparation activities are in progress for the barge landing on the Cuyuní River and the permanent camp area, which are key initial infrastructures to support future development.

Gurupi Project Highlights

GMIN closed the acquisition of tenements in the Gurupi Gold Belt from a wholly owned subsidiaries of BHP in December 2024. In February 2025, GMIN announced an updated National Instrument 43-101 ("NI 43-101") compliant mineral resource estimate, totaling 1.83 Moz of indicated resources grading 1.31 g/t Au and 0.77 Moz of inferred resources grading 1.29 g/t Au (see news release dated February 20, 2025).

About G Mining Ventures Corp.

G Mining Ventures Corp. is a mining company engaged in the acquisition, exploration and development of precious metal projects to capitalize on the value uplift from successful mine development. GMIN is well-positioned to grow into the next mid-tier precious metals producer by leveraging strong access to capital and proven development expertise. GMIN is currently anchored by the Tocantinzinho Mine in Brazil, supported by the Gurupi Project in Brazil and the Oko West Project in Guyana — all with significant exploration upside and located in mining-friendly jurisdictions.

data.title: Himalaya Shipping Ltd. (HSHP) Notice of 2025 Annual General Meeting
data.description:
Hamilton, Bermuda, March 28, 2025

Himalaya Shipping Ltd. (the “Company”) has previously announced on February 27, 2025 that it has scheduled its 2025 Annual General Meeting for May 21, 2025. A copy of the Notice of Annual General Meeting and Form of Proxy, and associated information including the 2024 Annual Report on Form 20-F is attached to this press release and can be found on the Company’s website at www.himalaya-shipping.com.

data.title: Goldstone Resources to Issue Shares for Loan Interest Settlement
data.description:
Goldstone Resources said Friday that Asian Investment Management Services, or AIMSL, agreed to receive new shares to settle 247.72 troy ounces of accrued interest under their gold loan agreement.

The mining and development company will issue 49,003,680 new ordinary shares, which represent the balance of the 101,803,680 conversion shares due to AIMSL.

Following the share issuance, the outstanding balance under the agreement will be 1,871.43 troy ounces, while the outstanding accrued interest stands at 395.20 troy ounces.
